standard_description: Explain the function of nouns, pronouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs in general and their functions in particular sentences.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Common and proper nouns
khan_exercises.description: Learn to distinguish common and proper nouns from one another!

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence are proper nouns?**

>The penguins had always wanted to go to Malaysia.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Proper nouns are the names given to specific people, places, or things.  They're always capitalized.
**"Malaysia"** is the name of a country, so it's a proper noun.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence are proper nouns?**

>Billy cautiously put the eggs in his basket.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Proper nouns are the names of specific people, places, and things.  They're always capitalized.
**"Billy"** is the name of a person, so it's a proper noun.

The **"Bone Wars"** is the name of a particular historical event, so it's a proper noun.

khan_exercises.title: Concrete and abstract nouns
khan_exercises.description: Learn to distinguish between abstract and concrete nouns!

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**

>My dreams often feature squirrels.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"
You could touch a squirrel, so **"squirrels"** is a concrete noun.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**


>Fiona took careful aim with her slingshot.

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"

You can touch a person, so a name like **"Fiona"** is a concrete noun.

You can touch a **"slingshot"**, so it's a concrete noun too.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**


>Harrison had a sudden realization.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"
You can touch a person, so a name like **"Harrison"** is a  concrete noun.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**

>Kyle found his inspiration in the desert.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"

You can touch a person, so a name like **"Kyle"** is a concrete noun.

A **"desert"** is a physical place you can be and touch, so it's a concrete noun.

items.question_content:
**Which of the nouns in this sentence is a concrete noun?**

>The bears were cranky after their hibernation.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Stuff that you could physically **touch** (like a mountain, a person, or the moon) is **concrete.**

Stuff that you **couldn’t touch** (like freedom, bravery, or friendship) is **abstract.**

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"
A bear is something you could touch (though doing so might not be wise), so **"bears"** is a concrete noun.

So, for each noun, ask yourself, "Could I touch it?"

You can touch a person, so a name like **"Natalie"** is a concrete noun.

A **"forest"** is a physical place you can be and touch, so it's a concrete noun.

khan_exercises.title: Indefinite pronouns
khan_exercises.description: Which indefinite pronoun makes the most sense for each of these sentences?
topic_path: Indefinite pronouns, pronoun vagueness, and emphatic pronouns

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>“Which do you prefer, horses or giraffes?”

“I like them  \_\_\_\_\_\_ equally.”

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
The question is asking a question about a preference between _two_ things, horses and giraffes, so you will be using one of the *dual* indefinite pronouns.
Because we're talking about two things at the same time (and not one or the other), we should use **"both"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>You can pick only one of these prizes. You can take  \_\_\_\_\_\_ the ball or the yo-yo.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
The question is asking a question about a preference between _two_ things, a ball or a yo-yo, so you will be using one of the *dual* indefinite pronouns.
Because we're choosing just *one* of two things, we should use the pronoun **"either"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

> \_\_\_\_\_\_ was admiring Maria Paula’s shiny, eye-catching cape.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
You will need to use a *singular* pronoun to agree with the singular verb "was."
We should use the singular pronoun **"everyone"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>\_\_\_\_\_\_ waited for me to finish getting ready. By the time I finally made it to the car,  they were all pretty annoyed.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
We need an indefinite pronoun that can make sense with the plural "they".
The only choice that could refer to multiple people is **"everyone"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>“Would you like to try the steak or the macaroni and cheese?”

“ \_\_\_\_\_\_, thank you; I’m not hungry.”

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_

The question is asking a question about a preference between _two_ things, steak or macaroni and cheese, so you will be using one of the *dual* indefinite pronouns. You will choose a pronoun that indicates you do not want anything to eat.
Because we're choosing *none* of two things, we should use the negative dual pronoun **"neither"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>If you don’t want to go on the roller coasters, we can find  \_\_\_\_\_\_ else to do.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
The pronoun is going to be singular in this sentence.
We should use the singular pronoun **"something"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>*The Knights of Vamore* was K.L. Price’s first book, but she has written  \_\_\_\_\_\_ others since.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
The pronoun will be plural in this sentence to agree with the plural "others."
We should use the plural pronoun **"many"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>I’m hoping  \_\_\_\_\_\_ can help me with this math homework.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
The pronoun will be singular in this sentence. The speaker is looking for a person to help them.
We should use the pronoun **"someone"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>I invited my friends to come over tonight. But since they all had concert tickets, \_\_\_\_\_\_ showed up.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
We need a pronoun that can refer to the *absence* of people.
We should use the negative pronoun **"nobody"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

> \_\_\_\_\_\_ people are early risers, while others prefer to stay up late at night.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
The pronoun will be plural in this sentence to agree with the noun "people."
Since we're not referring to *all* people, we should use the pronoun **"some"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>Has anybody seen my stuffed parrot? I’ve been looking for it and can’t find it  \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
Choose a pronoun that shows the speaker has been unable to find the parrot in the places they've looked.
Since the sentence is suggesting that *no* place has the parrot, we should use the pronoun **"anywhere"**.

items.question_content:

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
A singular pronoun is needed here to refer to a thing or idea.
We should use the singular pronoun **"nothing"**.

>\_\_\_\_\_\_ of my friends enjoy going to the zoo.

[[☃ radio 1]]

- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
A plural pronoun is needed here to match with the plural verb "enjoy".
We should use the plural pronoun **"most"**.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
A singular pronoun is needed here.
We should use the singular pronoun **"anything"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>That box of cookies was delicious! I’d like to buy  \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
A singular pronoun is needed to agree with "box."
We should use the singular pronoun **"another"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>My pet chipmunk doesn’t bite! You have  \_\_\_\_\_\_ to worry about.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
A singular pronoun is needed here. The speaker is assuring the listener that they don't have anything to fear.
We should use the singular negative pronoun **"nothing"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!

>We’ve temporarily run out of pizza, but don’t worry:  \_\_\_\_\_\_ is coming!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
This sentence needs a singular pronoun to agree with the singular "is."
We should use the singular pronoun **"more"**.

items.question_content:
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun for this sentence!


>\_\_\_\_\_\_ Esenia and Alisa work at the bike shop.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Indefinite pronouns are undefined; they can be about **anything.** 
 
- They can be both subjects and objects of a sentence
- _both, either,_ and _neither_ refer to things in twos.  This is called the *dual case*.
- They are usually treated as singular (except for _both, many, others, several,_ and sometimes _some_)

Examples: _any, anyone, everyone, both, neither, either, more, one, nothing, anything_
The sentence references *two* people, we need a pronoun that matches.
Because we're talking about two things at the same time (and not one or the other), we should use **"both"**.

khan_exercises.title: Pronoun vagueness
khan_exercises.description: Help clarify the sentence by keeping or changing existing pronouns.
topic_path: Indefinite pronouns, pronoun vagueness, and emphatic pronouns

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Rebecca told Lauren that Abby said __she__ was the best trombonist in school.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"She" could refer to Rebecca, Lauren, or Abby. 

To make the sentence clear, we should replace "she" with a specific noun.
"The girl" or "that one" could still refer to any of the girls, so we should use the name of the particular girl, **"Lauren"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Carlos and Matt were strolling along the boardwalk when __he__ slipped on a banana peel.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "He" could refer to Carlos or to Matt. Which choice clarifies "he"?
"The boy" or "one" could still refer to either boy, so we should be more specific and say **"Carlos"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Pamela and Amy stopped at a gas station so __she__ could buy some snacks.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "She" could refer to Pamela or to Amy. Which choice clarifies "she"?
"The hungry one" and "that girl" could still refer to either girl, so we should be more specific and say **"Amy"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Roxana found Mei’s soccer ball in __her__ backyard.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "Her" could refer to Roxana or to Mei. Which choice clarifies "her"?
"That" and "the girl's" could still refer to either girl, so we should be more specific and say **"Roxana's"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Deirdre told Winifred she would read __her__ favorite book.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "Her" could refer to Deirdre or to Winifred. Which choice clarifies "her"?
"The" and "a" do not clarify whose favorite book is being referred to, so we should be more specific and say **"Winnifred's"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>When Diego approached the koala, __he__ became distressed.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "He" could refer to Diego or to the koala. Which choice clarifies "he"?
"One of them" and "the male" could still refer to either Diego or the koala, so we should be more specific and say **"the koala"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Although Leslie and Cherise both love peanuts, __she__ rarely has peanut butter in her house.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "She" could refer to Leslie or to Cherise. Which choice clarifies "she"?
"One of them" and "the woman" could still refer to either woman, so we should be more specific and say **"Leslie"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Saoirse asked her mother if she had seen __her__ missing sweatshirt.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "Her" could refer to Saoirse or to her mother. Which choice clarifies "her"?
"That one's" and "the woman's" could still refer to either woman, so we should be more specific and say **"Saoirse's"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Ms. Evans and Shira talked about how __she__ would be out of town for a few weeks.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "Her" could refer to Ms. Evans or to Shira. Which choice clarifies "her"?
"The woman" or "one" could still refer to either woman, so we should be more specific and say **"the latter"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>The waiter approached Javier and Hector’s table and asked how __he__ was enjoying the steak.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "He" could refer to Javier or Hector. Which choice clarifies "he"?
"The patron" and "one of them" could still refer to either man, so we should be more specific and say **"Hector"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Marco and his father worked together to turn the old storage room into __his__ office.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "His" could refer to Marco or to his father. Which choice clarifies "his"?
"The man's" and "one's" could still refer to either man, so we should be more specific and say **"Marco's"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>The gorilla and the chimpanzee ran until __one of them__ got tired.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "One of them" could refer to the gorilla or to the chimpanzee. Which choice clarifies "one of them"?
"The animal" and "he" could still refer to either animal, so we should be more specific and say **"the gorilla"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Paula and Angelica rode the train; when an elderly man came aboard, __she__ gave up her seat.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "She" could refer to Paula or to Angelica. Which choice clarifies "she"?
"The woman" and "the rider" could still refer to either woman, so we should be more specific and say **"Paula"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Adriana asked if she could borrow Tara’s camera so Adriana could make a movie about __her.__ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "One of them" could refer to Adriana or to Tara. Which choice clarifies "one of them"?
"One" and "that girl" could still refer to either girl, so we should be more specific and say **"herself"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Andrea asked Olivia if she could come play chess at __her__ house.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "Her" could refer to Andrea or to her Olivia. Which choice clarifies "her"?
"The girl's" and "a" are still too ambiguous, , so we should be more specific and say **"Olivia's"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Ava told Madison she was glad that __she__ had joined the swim team.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "She" could refer to Ava or to Madison. Which choice clarifies "she"?
"The swimmer" and "one of them" could still refer to either girl, so we should be more specific and say **"Madison"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>On Luna and Maureen’s walk at the beach, __she__ stopped to pick up a mussel shell.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "She" could refer to Luna or to Maureen. Which choice clarifies "she"?
"The explorer" and "the woman" could still refer to either woman, so we should be more specific and say **"Luna"**.

items.question_content:
**It’s not clear who the __underlined pronoun__ in this sentence is referring to. Which choice makes the sentence clear?**

>Jeanine told Stefania she hoped __she__ would win first prize at the science fair.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: "She" could refer to Jeanine or to Stefania. Which choice clarifies "she"?
"The scientist" and "a student" could still refer to either girl, so we should be more specific and say **"Stefania"**.

khan_exercises.title: Emphatic pronouns
khan_exercises.description: Are these pronouns being used for emphasis, or does the sentence need them in order to make sense?
topic_path: Indefinite pronouns, pronoun vagueness, and emphatic pronouns

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>You don’t need to help me change this tire. I’ll do it __myself.__

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.

In this sentence, "I'll do it" and "I'll do it myself" would both make sense.

So, "myself" isn't necessary, it's just being **used for emphasis**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>Friederike found __herself__ singing along to every song on the radio.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.
If we removed the underlined pronoun, the sentence wouldn't make sense. So, **"herself" is needed**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>You’re going to Mount Kilimanjaro? I have never been,  __myself__.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.

In this sentence, "I have never been" and "I have never been, myself" would both make sense.

So, "myself" isn't necessary, it's just being **used for emphasis**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>We have an extra week to finish our book reports. Mrs. Kapur said so __herself.__

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.

In this sentence, "Mrs. Kapur said so" and "Mrs. Kapur said so herself" would both make sense.

So, "herself" isn't necessary, it's just being **used for emphasis**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>Réka has trouble sleeping, but she finds that telling stories to __herself__ helps.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.
If we removed the underlined pronoun, the sentence wouldn't make sense. So, **"herself" is needed**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>Join us for a special event with John Wilkerson, where he __himself__ will be signing copies of his latest book, The Ghost Train.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.

In this sentence, "he will be signing" and "he himself would be signing" would both make sense.

So, "himself" isn't necessary, it's just being **used for emphasis**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>The sisters were not invited to their friend’s weekend vacation, so they decided to take a trip __themselves.__

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.

In this sentence, "take a trip" and "take a trip themselves" would both make sense.

So, "themselves" isn't necessary, it's just being **used for emphasis**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>Rolling down that steep grassy hill was fun, but I ended up injuring __myself.__

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.
If we removed the underlined pronoun, the sentence wouldn't make sense. So, **"myself" is needed**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>If you can’t catch that chicken __yourself,__ I will help you.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.

In this sentence, "catch that chicken" and "catch that chicken yourself" would both make sense.

So, "yourself" isn't necessary, it's just being **used for emphasis**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the underlined pronoun is needed, or if it’s just being used for emphasis!**

>After our team won the baton race, we treated __ourselves__ to some ice cream.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **Emphatic pronouns** are just used for emphasis. If we eliminate an emphatic pronoun, the sentence will still make sense.
If we removed the underlined pronoun, the sentence wouldn't make sense. So, **"ourselves" is needed**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>You can’t expect me to carry all of these boxes  \_\_\_\_\_\_!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.
Of the choices, only **"myself"** is a reflexive pronoun that can be used for emphasis.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>Furaha’s birthday is coming up. Let’s throw a surprise party for  \_\_\_\_\_\_!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.

Because Furaha isn't throwing her own party, we shouldn't use a reflexive pronoun.

Instead, we should use the simple pronoun **"her"**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>Aurelie’s favorite time of the year is autumn, but I prefer the spring, \_\_\_\_\_\_ .

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.
Of the choices, only **"myself"** is a reflexive pronoun that can be used for emphasis.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>When Militza and Zofia decided to go to the zoo, it didn’t occur to  \_\_\_\_\_\_ that it would be closed.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.

Because the girls aren't the ones "occuring", we shouldn't use a reflexive pronoun.

Instead, we should use the simple pronoun **"them"**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>Do you know how to get  \_\_\_\_\_\_ to the train station?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.
Of the choices, only **"yourself"** is a reflexive pronoun that can be used for emphasis.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>The Governor  \_\_\_\_\_\_ welcomed her guests at the doorway.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.
Of the choices, only **"herself"** is a reflexive pronoun that can be used for emphasis.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>Do you know where Dushan is? It’s not like  \_\_\_\_\_\_ to run so late.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.

Because Dushan isn't doing something to/for himself, we shouldn't use a reflexive pronoun.

Instead, we should use the simple pronoun **"him"**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>The bears caught the salmon  \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.
Of the choices, only **"themselves"** is a reflexive pronoun that can be used for emphasis.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

> We made this casserole using what was already in  \_\_\_\_\_\_ refrigerator.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.
Because we're expressing possession of the refrigerator, we should use the possessive adjective **"our"**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct pronoun in this sentence!**

>You shouldn't have tried to fix the bike \_\_\_\_\_\_! I was  five minutes away!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emphatic pronouns are reflexive pronouns that add emphasis to a sentence.
Of the choices, only **"yourself"** is a reflexive pronoun that can be used for emphasis.
